coredata.dc:title: One-step synthesis of Mn-carbon dot nanoprobe for signal-on detection of arsenic and reversible temperature sensing
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Behera, Lingaraj
Pati, Debasish
Sahu, Binod Bihari
Mohapatra, Sasmita
coredata.dc:description: Manganese dioxide-carbon dot (MnO2CD) composite nanoparticles of size 45 nm have been synthesized in a single step from hydrothermal decomposition of KMnO4, polydopamine, and citric acid for multipurpose applications. The mild emission of MnO2CD particles at 425 nm under excitation wavelength of 340 nm substantially increases in the presence of arsenite, because of redox reaction between MnO2 and arsenite to give soluble Mn2+. This single probe sensor shows selective and sensitive response to arsenite in the linear range of 0–100 nM with a detection limit of 5.75 nM. This MnO2CD probe also shows satisfactory performance for detection of organoarsenic and organothioarsenic compounds such as dimethylarsenate (DMA), dimethyldithioarsinate (DMDTA), and dimethylmonothioarsinate (DMMTA). Interestingly, this sensor works well for monitoring arsenic in a plant root vascular system through fluorescence and confocal imaging which has been reported for the first time. Further, the fluorescence intensity of MnO2CD changes reversibly with temperature in the range of 25–75 °C. This nanobionic sensor not only opens up a universal opportunity to detect As(III) in environment as well in agriculture but also sensing temperature in a microenvironment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: MnO2CD
Fluorescence
Arsenic
Temperature sensor
Nanobionic sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence detection of arsenite, DMA, DMDTA, DMMTA
Detection of As in real water samples
Visual detection of arsenic in plant
Determination of arsenite in real samples
Application of MnO2 CD for visible detection of arsenic in rice and mung plant

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Authors acknowledge SERB , DST, grant no CRG/2018/001717 for financial support for this work. LB thanks NIT Rourkela for providing Ph. D. fellowship.

The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Sasmita Mohapatra reports financial support was provided by SERB.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of MnO2CD, (b) HRTEM image of MnO2CD showing lattice fringes, (c) EDX showing presence of C, O, and N, (d) XRD, (e) XPS survey spectrum of MnO2CD (f) High resolution XPS scan corresponding to Mn2p.
(a) PL response of MnO2CD towards As (b) linear response. Concentration of added MnO2CD solution is 0.1 mg/mL.
(a) selectivity of the MnO2CD probe towards As (b) response towards As in presence of other analytes. Concentration of added MnO2CD solution is 0.1 mg/mL.
(a) PL response of MnO2CD towards DMA, (b) DMMTA, (c) DMDTA (d) linear response of DMA, (e) DMMTA, (f) DMDTA. In all cases the concentration MnO2CD probe solution is 0.1 mg/mL.
(a) emission of MnO2CD w.r.t PCD (b) PL decay comparison between PCD and MnO2CD and after addition As with MnO2CD (c) HD size of MnO2CD towards As.
(a) PL response of MnO2CD with increase in temperature, (b) PL response of MnO2CD with decreasing temp, (c) linear response of MnO2CD with heating and cooling, (d) Reusability cycle.
(a-p) Confocal image of root sections of MnO2CD treated rice and moog plants preincubated with increasing arsenite concentration. Concentration of MnO2CD solution was 0.4 mg/mL in both plants.

(A-F) digital photo of rice root under UV lamp, (G) Linear response of FL intensity w.r.t. As concentration in rice root, (a-f) digital photo of mung sprout under UV lamp, (g) Linear response of FL intensity w.r.t. As concentration in mung sprout. In all cases MnO2CD probe concentration is 0.4 mg/mL.
Arsenite detection mechanism illustration by MnO2CD.
Analytical performance of MnO2CD fluorescence sensor in real samples.
Analytical performance of MnO2CD fluorescence sensor in real samples.
Detection of different organoarsenic compounds using MnO2CD nanosensor.

head.ce:title: One-step synthesis of Mn-carbon dot nanoprobe for signal-on detection of arsenic and reversible temperature sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, National Institute of Technology, Rourkela 769008, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, National Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, National Institute of Technology, Rourkela, India-769008

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Life Science, National Institute of Technology, Rourkela 769008, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Life Science, National Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Life Science, National Institute of Technology, Rourkela, India-769008

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Manganese dioxide-carbon dot (MnO2CD) composite nanoparticles of size 45 nm have been synthesized in a single step from hydrothermal decomposition of KMnO4, polydopamine, and citric acid for multipurpose applications. The mild emission of MnO2CD particles at 425 nm under excitation wavelength of 340 nm substantially increases in the presence of arsenite, because of redox reaction between MnO2 and arsenite to give soluble Mn2+. This single probe sensor shows selective and sensitive response to arsenite in the linear range of 0–100 nM with a detection limit of 5.75 nM. This MnO2CD probe also shows satisfactory performance for detection of organoarsenic and organothioarsenic compounds such as dimethylarsenate (DMA), dimethyldithioarsinate (DMDTA), and dimethylmonothioarsinate (DMMTA). Interestingly, this sensor works well for monitoring arsenic in a plant root vascular system through fluorescence and confocal imaging which has been reported for the first time. Further, the fluorescence intensity of MnO2CD changes reversibly with temperature in the range of 25–75 °C. This nanobionic sensor not only opens up a universal opportunity to detect As(III) in environment as well in agriculture but also sensing temperature in a microenvironment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In view of both biological and environmental aspects, detection of toxic ions with higher sensitivity and selectivity is a major research goal. Fast development of manufacturing industries, mining process, irrational human actions, use of arsenic-based herbicides and pesticides increase the toxicity level of groundwater[1]. In groundwater intrinsic toxicity of As(III) leads to various health effects such as respiratory disorders, cardiovascular, genotoxic, carcinogenic effects, etc [2,3]. Arsenic is found as arsenite (+3, H3AsO3) and arsenate (+5, H2AsO4 −, HAsO4 2−) [4]. Although arsenite (As3+, H3AsO3) is the most stable and mobile form of As species in a reducing condition, trace amounts of organoarsenates such as dimethylarsenate (DMA), monomethylarsenate (MMA) have been reported to be present in pore water of paddy soils which are potentially toxic [5]. Apart from this, toxicities of inorganic thioarsenates such as dimethyldithioarsenate (DMDTA), dimethylmonothioarsenate (DMMTA), which are produced from arsenite via OH− /SH−ligand exchange, are at par with that of inorganic trivalent As [6–8]. As per the World Health Organization (WHO) the accepted limit of As in drinking water is 10 ppb [9]. Therefore, accurate determination of As is essential. To date, sensitive detection of arsenic has been enormously carried out by conventional analytical methods like atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS)[10], Gas Chromatography (GC)[11], inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS)[12], atomic fluorescence spectrometry (AFS)[13], and so on. Although these methods have superior sensitivity, due to several demerits like multi-step pre-treatments, professional operation conditions, and lengthy procedures, the practical application of these methods is limited. Alternatively, the fluorescence technique provides fast, low-cost means for the visual detection of analytes [14,15]. In recent years with the emerging popularity of carbon dots, few carbon dot fluorescence nanosensors such as magnetic Fe-GQDs [16], amino-functionalized CDs [17], trithiocyanuric acid-modified gold nanoparticles (TMT-Au NPS) [18], polyaniline nanofiber modified CDs [19], dual mode optical sensors. have been reported for the detection and quantification of As(III) where the interactions of carbon dot-based probe and arsenite are based on defined photophysical processes like fluorescence quenching, aggregation-induced emission, inner filter effect, etc. Although significant numbers of research papers have been published on quantum dot based fluorescence methods for the detection of arsenic, the outcomes of most of these works are limited to the detection of inorganic arsenite (+3, H3AsO3) in water. Further, most of the sensors are based on turn-off sensing where environmental interference is a major drawback. Therefore, development of a versatile fluorescence nanobionic sensor for turn-on detection of arsenic as well as organoarsenic compounds with a great potential for arsenic imaging in biological environment is indispensable.

On the other hand, based on a wide absorption range (250 −500 nm) as well as a series of merits such as diverse enzymatic activities, biocompatibility, easy fabrication, and strong oxidation, MnO2 nanomaterial represent an attractive probe for fluorescence analysis. More specifically MnO2 and fluorescent quantum dots and/or organic molecules integrated systems such as MnO2-fluorescent CD [20], polydopamine-MnO2 [21], Upconversion nanoparticles-MnO2 [22], Au Np-MnO2 [23] have been explored for fluorescence signal off-on detection of glutathione, uric acid, organophosphorus pesticides, ascorbic acid, H2O2 and temperature respectively. However, in spite of substantial research in MnO2CD fluorescence sensor, the literature on the application of this integrated sensor on reducing inorganic ions like As(III) is sparse. Relying on the ability of manganese oxides to oxidize toxic As(III) ions to less toxic As (V) via electron transfer, a novel fluorescence sensor can be constructed by integrating MnO2 with CDs which achieve arsenite ion detection. Further, arsenite is efficiently adsorbed by plants through different mechanisms. However, there is lack of reliable techniques capable of rapidly assessing the uptake as well as translocation of arsenite in plants. Optical imaging of arsenite ions in plants using nanobionic sensor is of current importance to develop measures that reduce As uptake and toxicity in plants. The main criteria of a fluorescent probe for monitoring metal ions inside the plants must focus on high aqueous dispersion stability which results in easy translocation inside the plant and stable fluorescence of the probe. At the same time the sensor must work on turn-on increase in PL intensity rather than turn off so as to provide a small signal compared to a dark background.

In this paper, we have prepared uniform MnO2CD composite nanoparticle in a single step from hydrothermal decomposition of citric acid, polydopamine, and KMnO4. MnO2CDs possessed small particle size, low cytotoxicity, and showed feeble emission property. However, when comes in contact with arsenite, or organoarsenates such as DMA, DMDTA, DMMTA the fluorescence intensity of MnO2CD appreciably increases in a concentration-dependent manner. Interestingly, when applied in rice and mung plants, these MnO2CDs notably promote bean sprout growth while simultaneously used as a luminescent probe for detecting arsenic through fluorescence microscopy. This work provides a simple approach for synthesizing MnO2CD fluorescent nanoprobe and extends the new application of this nanoprobe in monitoring arsenic in plants. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first paper on the detection of arsenic poisoning in plants through fluorescence/confocal imaging. At the same time, the temperature-responsive luminescence property of this MnO2CD nanoprobe in the temperature range of 25–75 °C offers its potential to be used as a nanothermometer in physiological environments.

Dopamine hydrochloride (DA), citric acid (CA), potassium permanganate (KMnO4), Tri(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane (Tris), Dimethylarsenate (DMA) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Dimethylmonothioarsenic acid (DMMTA) and Dimethyldithioarsenic acid (DMDTA) have been prepared by using DMA as a precursor (SI). Sodium arsenite and other chemicals were procured from Merck Ltd. Milli-Q water (18.2 MΩ) were used throughout whole experimental procedures. The pH of the tris-Buffer solution in the experiment was adjusted by adding 0.1 molL-1 NaOH. Rice seed and Mung seed has been taken from the local market for plant experiment.

UV–visible absorption and fluorescence spectra were obtained using a UV–VIS spectrophotometer (Shimadzu 2650) and a spectrofluorimeter (Quantamaster, Horiba-PTI). Particle sizes data were obtained using a zeta sizer Nano ZS 90, Malvern instrument. The Raman spectrum was obtained using a Raman spectrometer (Renishaw, UK model). Surface functional groups was determined by utilizing Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) (IRAffinity-1S, Shimadzu, spectrophotometer). Morphology and distribution of elements in the sample were investigated using a Transmission electron microscope (FEI, Tecnai G2 TF30-ST). The surface composition of MnO2CD was examined by X-ray photoelectron spectrum (XPS) recorded by (PHI 5000 VersaProbe III). Imaging of arsenic in rice and mung plants was done by a confocal microscope (Confocal TCS SP8, Leica).

40 mg dopamine hydrochloride was mixed with 8 mL of tris buffer (10 mM, pH 8.8) and kept in mixing for 24 h under room temperature to form polydopamine. In the second step, to synthesize MnO2CD, 0.78 g of CA was solubilized in a mixture of 6 mL polydopamine and 40 mL of Milli-Q water to obtain a black colour solution. This dark-colored solution was rapidly agitated for 30 min followed by the mixing of 0.023 g KMnO4 and stirred for 15 mins. Later, the prepared solution was poured into polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) autoclave heated for 24 h at 200 °C. When the solution mixture was cooled to normal temperature automatically, large particles formed in the solution were decanted out by centrifuging at a speed of 12,000 rpm for 10 min, and brownish-yellow color supernatant was kept for further filtration. The supernatant part was filtered thrice with a 0.22 µm membrane filter and transferred to a dialysis bag (MW = 500). By exchanging the water for 24 h to get pure CD solution through dialysis process. In the final step, the dialysis solution was lyophilized and kept in a vacuum for up to 72 h. Finally, the water-dispersible brown powder was collected and kept in 4 °C for next use.

Various reaction conditions like reaction temperatures, and time were optimized to reach the best fluorescence property of synthesized MnO2CD. The ratio of CA:KMnO4 has been maintained as 1:010 for synthesis of MnO2CD. The temperature and time have been optimized and fixed at 200 °C for 24 h to get MnO2CD with best fluorescence property (SI).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of arsenite, DMA, DMDTA, DMMTA
ce:section.ce:para: Arsenite stock solutions ranging from 0 to 600 nM were mixed with 40 µL of the aqueous dispersion of MnO2CD (0.1 mg/mL) and incubated at 25 °C for 15 min. The PL recovery spectra at various analyte concentrations at an excitation wavelength of 340 nm were recorded to calculate the sensitivity of MnO2CD toward arsenite. The selectivity study of the MnO2CD was examined by mixing individually with other ions, like Cu2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Ca2+, Cr3+, Ba2+, Hg2+, Co2+, K+, NO3-, SO4 2-, ClO4-, Cl-, Br-, PO4 3- etc. following the same process. The interference of other cations/anions in the FL spectrum of MnO2CD in presence of arsenite was done by adding 20 µM of interfering cation or anions in the sensing medium. Similarly, different concentration of DMA, DMDTA, DMMTA stock solutions ranging from 0 to 200 µM were mixed with 40 µL of aqueous dispersion of MnO2CD in water followed by incubation at room temperature up to 0.5 h. PL spectra were recorded under λex 340 nm. The fluorescence spectra of arsenite solutions (0–200 nM) incubated with MnO2CD of different concentrations (0.5–1.5 mg/mL) were also recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of As in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: Water samples, like pond water (NIT Rourkela campus), groundwater (Boring water), Bramhani River water of Odisha, and seawater were collected and a pretreatment was done to separate insoluble substances. After that, MnO2CD (0.1 mg/mL) solution was added to all real samples. Arsenite solutions of various concentrations such as 40, 80, 120 nM were spiked and incubated for 15 min. Fluorescence spectra were taken following. Later from the calibration plot, the concentration of arsenite in a real water sample was calculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual detection of arsenic in plant
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence images of plant specimens were captured under excitation of a UV lamp (365 nm). Then all the pictures were taken using the SAMSUNG M51 device. During the photoshoot, the position, angle, and direction of plants were kept constant. A slight change in the positioning of a plant may result in a negligible effect in photographs and results which will be under an accurate range of relative standard deviation (RSD). Optical photographs taken were examined with Image J software by following steps described in supportive information.

Herein, citric acid and polydopamine were selected as potential precursor materials for the synthesis of CD hydrothermally. As such pure CDs (PCD) emit blue emission and doping with metal ions quenches its fluorescence intensity. The yellow colour PCD solution gives two notable peaks at 232, 280 nm. The strong absorption peak at 232 nm was allocated to the π-π * transition of the carbogenic core and an intense peak that appears at 280 nm could be allocated to the n − π * transition of the functional heteroatom surface. The peak centered at 280 nm is a characteristic peak of polydopamine. Under 340 nm excitation, the maximum PL emission intensity of PCD was obtained. Moreover, PCD shown a good blue fluorescence emission at 425 nm under excitation at 340 nm. Absolute fluorescence quantum yield of PCD is measured to be 12 % at an excitation of 340 nm. TEM image shows PCDs are spherical monodisperse and have a mean average size of 18 nm [24]. Over the surface of PCD, FTIR spectra were used to identify the presence of various functional groups. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) indicated a broad and intense peak in the range of 3481–3150 cm-1confirming the presence of surface N-H/O-H Stretching vibration [25]. The strong peak located at 1625 cm-1 shows the existence of CC[26]. The peak located at 1325 cm-1 was due to the C-N stretching vibration in the PCD[27]. PCDs possessed a broad peak at 22.7 degrees indicating (002) plane confirming that the prepared carbon dot is amorphous [28].

In comparison with PCD, MnO2CD had shown equal stability, good absorbance, and weak fluorescence intensity. The hydrodynamic size of MnO2CD is less than 50 nm (Fig. S1). TEM image shows uniform MnO2CD with a size diameter of approximately 10 nm ( Fig. 1a). Close observation reveals that MnO2 is uniformly distributed across the carbon dot surface. The high-resolution TEM image shows lattice spacing is calculated to be 0.35 nm which is ascribed to the (311) plane of MnO2 [29]. The interplanar distance of 0.27 nm corresponds to (002) layers of graphitic carbon (Fig. 1b)[30]. Apart from that, the MnO2 is found to be mainly composed of C, N, O, and Mn (Fig. 1c). In the XRD pattern, two peaks centered at 38° and 68° embedded in the carbon, which could lead to a stronger synergistic effect between these two components. The XRD spectrum confirms the formation of monoclinic k-birnessite MnO2 (JCPDS no.42-1169)[31]. The presence of the amorphous PCD is revealed by the broad peak around 22.7°. In the Raman spectrum, two broad peaks at 1332 cm−1 and 1557 cm−1 were ascribed to the D and G band of MnO2CD assigned to the disordered and graphitic carbon. The extent of disorder and functionalization was found from the integrated intensity ratio ID/IG as 0.85. Furthermore, peak around 568 cm-1 is due to the presence of Mn−O stretching vibration (Fig. S2)[32]. The FTIR spectrum confirmed the presence of various surface functional groups in as-synthesized MnO2CD. MnO2CD exhibit peaks at 504 cm-1 due to Mn-O stretching vibration confirming the presence of MnO2 [33]. The band close to 3281–3150 cm-1 region has been allocated to O-H and N-H stretching vibration and 1625 cm-1 are ascribed CC stretching vibration, respectively (Fig. S3) [34]. XPS gives further insight into the surface composition and functional moiety of MnO2CD. From the survey spectrum and high-resolution scan peaks at 643.3 eV and 653.7 eV were allocated to the binding energy of Mn 2p3/2 and Mn 2p1/2 and an energy separation of 10.4 eV is indicative of Mn 2p3/2 and Mn 2p1/2 in MnO2 ( Fig. 2) [35]. The main peaks of Mn can be divided into two subpeaks corresponding Mn (IV) and Mn (III) of which Mn (IV) dominates, proving that the synthesized material is mainly MnO2(Fig. 1e, f). Deconvolution of C1s spectrum confirms that carbon is present in four different environments C-O, C-CO, CC, CO. Similarly, deconvolution of O1s spectrum confirms presence of oxygen in three different chemical environments, i.e., Mn-O-Mn, CO, Mn-OH (Fig. S4a, b) [36,37]. From deconvolution of N, it was confirmed that N1s peak at 399.5 eV and 397.3 eV binding energy refers to N-H and C-N-C bond (Fig. S4c) [38]. All of these spectroscopic analysis results evidence that MnO2 has been produced in the hydrothermal synthesis process in the CD clusters forming MnO2-CD.

The absorption spectrum of MnO2CD shows two peaks around 232 and 280 nm, and an extended tail up to 600 nm. Peaks at 232 and 280 nm corresponded to π−π*transition of sp2 carbon core and n−π*transition of sp3 carbon surface as well as heteroatomic surface functionalities (Fig. 2). It is found that the supernatant is nonfluorescent. When we optimized MnO2CD, by varying citric acid weight percentage with temperature and time it shows maximum intensity at 200 °C temperature and 24 h time (Fig S5). Absolute fluorescence quantum yield of MnO2CD is measured to be 5.14 % at an excitation wavelength 340 nm. The excitation dependent emission behaviour is particularly advantageous in bioimaging applications (Fig S6). The emission spectra of synthesized MnO2CDs have been recorded under variable concentrations of NaCl to investigate the ionic strength effect towards emission of MnO2CDs. From Fig. S7 no change in PL spectra was observed even at high concentration (2.5 M) of NaCl and clarified very high stability of MnO2CDs at different ionic strength conditions. From Fig. S8, with changing pH conditions no alteration of peak position was observed but emission intensity of MnO2CDs was slightly low. Over pH 5–9 no prominent variation in emission intensity was observed and this result can be beneficial for the luminescent probe to be used in the practical bioimaging applications.

The response of MnO2CDs toward arsenite ion was analyzed from PL intensity of aqueous dispersion of MnO2CD in variable concentrations of arsenite. It is shown that with an increase in arsenite concentration, the fluorescence intensity of MnO2CDs gradually increases at 425 nm ( Fig. 3a). Change in PL intensity of MnO2CDs has a linear relationship with arsenite concentration within 0–100 nM with a correlation coefficient of 0. 996. Also, the regression equation is y = 0.0131x+ 1.1095. The detection limit (LOD) of MnO2CDs toward arsenite ion was measured to be 5.75 nM(0.76ppb) using LOD formula 3 SD/ῤ, where SD refers to standard deviation and ῤ slope of linear regression curve (Fig. 3b)[39]. However, when arsenite was incubated MnO2CD solutions of higher concentration (0.5–1.5 mg/mL) no linear response was observed (Fig. S9) even though there was increase in fluorescence intensity in the range of 0–200 nM. Table 1 compares the performance of other fluorescence-based sensors with MnO2CD integrated sensors towards sensing arsenite. It is observed that the MnO2CD probe prepared by one-step hydrothermal synthesis works better as compared to other reported methods.

To determine the response of MnO2CDs probe towards other cations and anions, we compared PL response of MnO2CD towards arsenite with PL response of MnO2CD towards a series of cations and anions (such as Cu2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Ca2+, Cr3+, Ba2+, Hg2+, Co2+, K+, NO3-, SO4 2-, ClO4-, Cl-, Br-, PO4 3-). It was found that in case of arsenite, the turn-on increase in intensity is highest as compared to other cations and anions ( Fig. 4a). Further interfering test was carried out by adding arsenite solution and other interfering ions into MnO2CDs solution. It was observed that the equivalent concentration of interfering ions with arsenite and 10 times more concentration of other ions rarely influenced PL intensity of sensor (Fig. 4b). The selectivity towards As was also observed when MnO2CD probe was used at higher concentration (Fig. S10).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of arsenite in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The feasibility of the sensing process was investigated in four different real water samples due to the high selectivity and sensitivity of MnO2CD probes. For this purpose, river water, tap water, pond water, and seawater have been spiked with arsenite standard solutions. Non-spiked real sample has been taken as reference. For each water sample, the calibration curve was plotted using standard 40 nM, 80 nM, and 120 nM arsenite solutions in the same sample. The recovery percentage was calculated for a wide range of concentrations, as shown in Table 2. The results revealed that analytical recoveries of arsenite added to water samples ranged from 99% to 103.2%, indicating that current fluorescence detection has a high degree of accuracy and can be used to detect arsenite in real samples.

The fluorescence response of MnO2CDs toward organoarsenic compounds like DMA, DMMTA, DMDTA was analyzed following same sensing protocol. From Fig. 5(a-c) shows that with an increase in concentration of all the organoarsenic compounds, the fluorescent intensity (λ340) of MnO2CDs gradually increases at 425 nm. The increase in PL intensity of MnO2CDs has a linear relationship with concentration in case of all three organoarsenic compounds in the concentration range 0–200 µL with a correlation coefficient of 0.99, 0.97, 0.98. Detection limit of MnO2CDs towards all three compounds was calculated to be 4.39, 62.9, 32.9 µM by using LOD formula 3 SD/ῤ, where SD refers to standard deviation and ῤ slope of linear regression curve (Fig. 5d-f). The MnO2CD integrated probe has demonstrated a substantial turn on response to all arsenic compounds ( Table 3) with long linear response ranges and low detection limits.

In Scheme 1, the structure of the nanosensor and its mechanism for detecting arsenite are depicted. The CD derived from precursors CA and polydopamine shows an intense emission peak at 425 nm. When KMnO4 is added with the precursor during the synthesis process the PL intensity of the recovered brown-coloured particles (MnO2CD) decreases ( Fig. 6a). The CD formed in the medium reduces KMnO4 to MnO2 which leads to the formation of MnO2CD integrated particle. There was almost 7 times drop in PL intensity when the ratio of CA:KMnO4 has been changed from 1:002–1:0.01. The absorption peak of MnO2 extends over a broad region from 300 to 750 nm, thus there is a complete spectral overlap between emission of CD and MnO2 which gives rise to energy transfer from CD to MnO2. After addition of arsenite, MnO2 oxidizes As(III) to As(V) and converted into soluble Mn(II)[45]. Hence the MnO2CD structure is disintegrated which results in the regain of fluorescence. The re-establishment of the fluorescence signal of MnO2CD depends upon the reduction/oxidation reaction between the MnO2 and As(III) by the following equation.
(1) H3AsO3 + MnO2 +2 H+→H3AsO4 + Mn2+ + H2O

It was observed that PL intensity of MnO2CD enhances with the increase in As(III) concentration. To get more insight into the interaction of MnO2CD with arsenite, fluorescence lifetime studies was carried out at an excitation wavelength of 340 nm with their relevant emission wavelengths. The average lifetime (τav) of synthesized MnO2CD is 1.7 ns. In the presence of As(III), the excited state lifetime MnO2CD was found to be 4.5 ns (Fig. 6b). The increase in life time after addition of arsenite evidences the interruption in the energy transfer process which is a consequence of reduction of MnO2 to Mn2+. Measurement of hydrodynamic size of MnO2CD in presence of increasing concentration of arsenite further gives evidences in support of disintegration of MnO2CD composite nanoparticles to PCD and Mn2+. The HD size of MnO2CD in sensing medium reduces from 45 nm to 22 nm when arsenic concentration increases from 5 nM to 100 nM (Fig. 6c). In DMA, DMMTA, DMDTA As exist in + 5 oxidation state. It has been well documented that carbon dot acts as an electron donor as well as electron acceptor [46]. Thus, in case of organoarsenic compounds there may be transfer of electron from photoexcited carbon dot to As(V) which reduces As(V) to As (III). Further As3+reduces MnO2 to Mn2+ for which the MnO2CD probe is disintegrated and FL intensity is regained.

Although MnO2CD has less fluorescence intensity as compared to PCD, it can be used as a good temperature sensor. The feasibility of the MnO2CD sensor upon temperature dependency was examined by recording PL intensity with a variation of temperature. As manifested in Fig. 7a the PL intensity of MnO2CD decreases linearly with enhancing temperature from 25 oC to 75 °C, without any displacement of peak position. Finally, the fluorescence intensity was reduced by 30% of the original intensity at a temperature of 75 °C. Fig. 7b represents the linear relationship of PL peak intensity with temperature with correlation coefficient 0.972. To demonstrate the temperature-sensitive PL property's reversibility, the PL spectra of MnO2CD were measured again by decreasing the temperature range from 75 oC to 25 oC by cooling then PL intensity increases. Similarly, change in cooling temperature also shows a better linear relationship with a correlation coefficient of 0.974 (Fig. 7c). These findings show that MnO2CD PL intensities are reversible in both forward and backward temperature-changing processes. Furthermore, because of heating and cooling cycles, the reproducibility and stability of PL intensity are crucial for possible biological applications. As a result, repeated heating and cooling experiments between 25 and 75 °C were carried out. The PL intensity is reversible with inappreciable fluctuation at the same temperature (Fig. 7d). Therefore, MnO2CD shows large potential in temperature sensing because of predominant temperature-responsive PL property.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of MnO2 CD for visible detection of arsenic in rice and mung plant
ce:section.ce:para: The uptake and translocation conditions of MnO2CD in plants was studied by fluorescence imaging. The presence of MnO2CD inside the stem of rice root can be identified in vivo due to reasonable blue emissions from the MnO2CD, which can be observed under blue filter 405 nm excitation (Fig. S11), whereas no such intense blue emission was detected from the roots in the control group treated with pure water. The confocal images visually show that the MnO2CD luminescence signals were primarily found in the vascular system in root parts, which is further supported by confocal images of root longitudinal sections. It can be clearly seen that fluorescence of MnO2CD concentrated along the axially elongated vascular tissues in the parts of root and stem in both rice and mung plants. ( Fig. 8a-n) However, there is manifold increase in fluorescence signal of MnO2CD inside the roots of both plants preincubated with arsenite solution as observed from both fluorescence and confocal images Fig. 9(a-f). The PL intensity of the MnO2CD nanobiohybrids toward As3+ was linear (r = 0.991) when calculated in the range 0.02 − 10 μM of As3+ (Fig. 9g) and limit of detection is 2.04 µM. Similarly, in mung sprouts the quenched fluorescence intensity of MnO2CD also increases by adding arsenite (Fig. 9a-f) with a linear coefficient of 0.987 when calculated in range of 0.02–10 μM (Fig. 9g). The limit of detection of arsenite in mung plants has been calculated to be 2.90 µM.

MnO2CD nanoprobe has been synthesized via facile one-step hydrothermal synthesis using KMnO4 as Mn precursor. The probe shows feeble fluorescence at 425 nm due to energy transfer between CD and MnO2. Due to redox reaction between MnO2 and arsenic, MnO2CD probe is decomposed to CD and Mn2+in the presence of arsenic. The probe shows selective and sensitive detection towards arsenite and organoarsenic compounds like DMA, DMDTA, DMMTA. Further, MnO2CD shows good translocation properties in side rice and mung plants and can be potentially used for detection of arsenic deposition in plant roots visually under a fluorescence lamp as well as through microscopy. The temperature sensitive fluorescence property of MnO2CD probe establish its potential to be used as a nanothermometer in physiological environment.

Lingaraj Behera is responsible for all experimental work, data analysis and original draft of the manuscript. Debashis Pati has conducted plat experiments under the supervision of Dr. Binod Bihari Sahu. Dr. S. Mohapatra is responsible for conceptualization, writing, review and correction of the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Sasmita Mohapatra reports financial support was provided by SERB.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors acknowledge SERB, DST, grant no CRG/2018/001717 for financial support for this work. LB thanks NIT Rourkela for providing Ph. D. fellowship.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2022.130002.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
